article.title: Microsoft Agent Framework 1.0 Ships, Succeeding Semantic Kernel and AutoGen as the Company's Unified Enterprise SDK
article.summary: Microsoft's Agent Framework 1.0 consolidates Semantic Kernel and AutoGen into a single production-ready SDK for building multi-agent AI systems in .NET and Python.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Microsoft shipped Agent Framework 1.0 on April 3, 2026, ending more than six months of preview releases and positioning a new unified SDK as the company's canonical choice for building enterprise AI agents in .NET and Python. The 1.0 milestone marks the framework's graduation to stable APIs and a long-term support commitment.

The release comes as agent-based AI systems have moved from experimental curiosity to production priority across the enterprise software industry, and as Microsoft faces increasingly direct competition from Google's Agent Development Kit, OpenAI's Agents SDK, and LangGraph.

## What We Know

### A Unified SDK From Two Major Projects

Agent Framework 1.0 consolidates the foundations of two separate Microsoft open-source efforts. As [InfoQ noted](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/02/ms-agent-framework-rc/) when the Release Candidate shipped in February 2026, the framework "succeeds earlier efforts such as Semantic Kernel and AutoGen, consolidating agent creation, orchestration primitives, and multi-provider support under a single SDK." Semantic Kernel had provided enterprise-grade plugin infrastructure and model connectors; AutoGen, originally developed by Microsoft Research, contributed the multi-agent orchestration patterns that drew its large following.

[Microsoft's official 1.0 announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/agent-framework/microsoft-agent-framework-version-1-0/) urges teams to migrate: "Coming from AutoGen or Semantic Kernel? Now is the time to migrate to Microsoft Agent Framework," with migration assistants available to analyze existing code and generate step-by-step migration plans.

Semantic Kernel's GitHub repository currently holds more than 27,000 stars and AutoGen's more than 57,000 — a combined community footprint that Agent Framework is now positioned to absorb. The new unified repository itself has grown to nearly 10,000 stars since the April 3 general availability, with more than 120 contributors to the project.

### What Ships in 1.0

The framework's stable API surface covers several core areas, as described in [Microsoft's release post](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/agent-framework/microsoft-agent-framework-version-1-0/):

- **Multi-provider connectors**: First-party integrations for Microsoft Foundry, Azure OpenAI, OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, Amazon Bedrock, Google Gemini, and Ollama.
- **Graph-based workflow engine**: Supports sequential and concurrent agent orchestration, with checkpointing for long-running processes and human-in-the-loop scenarios.
- **Multi-agent orchestration patterns**: Derived from Microsoft Research's work, including handoff, group chat, and Magentic-One collaboration models.
- **Pluggable memory**: Providers for conversational history, persistent state, and vector-based retrieval without requiring external databases.
- **Middleware pipeline**: Interception hooks for logging, content safety, and compliance needs.
- **YAML-based declarative agents**: For teams that prefer configuration-driven agent definitions alongside the programmatic API.

The framework also ships with a browser-based DevUI debugger that visualizes agent execution, message flows, tool calls, and orchestration decisions in real time — a feature inherited and expanded from AutoGen's original debugging tooling.

### Protocol-Level Interoperability

A central emphasis in the 1.0 release is open-protocol support. The framework integrates with [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/foundry/from-local-to-production-the-complete-developer-journey-for-building-composing-and-deploying-ai-agents/), allowing agents to dynamically discover and invoke tools from any MCP-compliant server. It also includes the foundations for Agent-to-Agent (A2A) messaging — a structured, protocol-driven approach to cross-runtime agent collaboration — with fuller A2A 1.0 support announced as forthcoming.

According to the [Microsoft Foundry blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/foundry/from-local-to-production-the-complete-developer-journey-for-building-composing-and-deploying-ai-agents/), the framework integrates directly with Azure Developer CLI for deployment, enabling single-command promotion from local development to production-hosted agents with session isolation, sub-100-millisecond startup times, and VNET support.

### Background and RC Period

Microsoft first previewed Agent Framework at a developer event in October 2025. By February 2026, the project reached Release Candidate status, with [InfoQ noting](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/02/ms-agent-framework-rc/) that the API surface was "stable and feature-complete for what is planned in version 1.0." The RC period surfaced feedback that drove several breaking changes before GA. The [python-1.0.0 release notes](https://github.com/microsoft/agent-framework/releases) document the removal of deprecated `BaseContextProvider` and `BaseHistoryProvider` aliases as well as changes to the `Message` constructor API, which dropped the legacy `text` parameter in favor of a `contents` list.

The project has moved quickly since the April 3 general availability. [Release notes on GitHub](https://github.com/microsoft/agent-framework/releases) show python-1.1.0 shipped April 21, adding a `GeminiChatClient` for Google Gemini API and Vertex AI support and initial A2A metadata propagation. Python-1.2.0 followed on April 24, adding the first Agent Framework-to-A2A bridge and OpenTelemetry integration for GitHub Copilot agents. The .NET packages have tracked a parallel release cadence over the same period.

## What We Don't Know

Several significant questions remain open. Language support is a known gap — the 1.0 release covers .NET and Python only, with no announced timeline for Java, Go, TypeScript, or Rust. For many enterprise teams outside the Microsoft stack, this limits immediate adoption.

It is also unclear how far the AutoGen community will follow Microsoft's migration path rather than fork the project independently. AutoGen's GitHub discussion thread following the consolidation announcement showed some interest in community-maintained alternatives, though no major fork has emerged publicly.

The A2A integration, while referenced prominently in the 1.0 announcement, remains incomplete. Microsoft has described A2A 1.0 interoperability as "coming soon," and the broader ecosystem of A2A-compatible agents needed to make cross-framework agent collaboration practical is still nascent. Independent analysts have suggested it may take another year or two before A2A adoption reaches the scale that would make it a meaningful differentiator.

Finally, Microsoft's roadmap for tighter integration with Microsoft 365 Copilot and Teams — referenced in Foundry documentation — has not been detailed publicly beyond what is available in early preview. The commercial implications of Agent Framework becoming the backbone of enterprise Copilot customization could significantly shape its adoption curve.

## What Comes Next

For the .NET and Python communities, Agent Framework 1.0 represents the clearest path Microsoft has offered to date for building AI agents inside existing enterprise infrastructure. The framework's explicit design around open protocols positions it to interoperate with the broader agent ecosystem rather than lock teams into a Microsoft-only stack — at least in principle.

The real test will be whether enterprise development teams, many of whom built significant investment on top of Semantic Kernel or AutoGen, migrate at the pace Microsoft expects, or whether the consolidation accelerates adoption of competing frameworks that do not require an architectural transition.
